sentences.text: Well, the door was open.
I was the first one to get out.
They put ramps on the-- on the trucks, or near the trucks.
I was the first one to get out.
Everybody was trying to push and push and shove and things like that.
And, well, we saw-- as a matter of fact, it was nighttime.
Trains almost most of the time arrived at nighttime.
And we saw dobermans and shepherds and SS and screaming and shouting, full of the whole-- ramp was full.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they told us, raus, raus, raus, and we went out and we stood there.
And on the other trains, they were unloading the luggage, as well.
We noticed that.
So we had to ride up, and the women with the children, we are now separate, and the man separate, and so on and so on and so on.
So the whole ramp was full because the train was about, what?
There were about 12 wagons or 15 wagons there, and each wagon took about 50 people or more.
Even more wagons.
And we saw guys in stripes, and I couldn't work out what that they were.
They were helping.
They were helping people to get out and-- and I was talking to them, and--
I was talking to them first in German, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then I heard they were [INAUDIBLE] and things like that, you know?
And some, I heard them talk in Yiddish.
So, you know, I couldn't speak Yiddish very well, because I never learned Yiddish.
I only learned Yiddish in the camp itself.
I never spoke Yiddish before.
They spoke back to me to be careful, and this and this, and they were trying to tell peoples a few things, you know?
Some people-- but they couldn't talk to the people.
They were too excited to listen.
Well, I heard that we are here in a-- in a-- in a camp and things like that, and don't go right, don't go left, you know.
Things like that.
And in the meantime, everybody was helping everybody else.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I got the situation that the people go in the crema-- they talked to us about crematoriums and this and this.
Those-- those fellows with the striped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And try to get to work.
Try to get to work.
That's all they're telling.
Try to work.
And then there was a selection on, you know?
High ranking officers from the SS, Obersturmfuhrers and Sturmbannfuhrers.
Because I knew their signs.
I could read-- I could read every German rank.
I made it my business when I was before to identify them.
And they were there with their gloves and their boots and their [INAUDIBLE] and their dogs and their wi-- and their whips.
And they were saying, what are you doing?
So, you know, our people, unfortunately.
And I said they didn't want to work, you see?
They thought they're going to work in the factories and the fields.
And I said, I'm sick, even if he wasn't sick.
I can't lift very well.
So-- That's what you heard people saying.
Mm-hm.
So he says, I've got a bad back.
So he says, we've got-- the fellow said to him, we got special places for people with bad backs.
You go to the right.
The other one said, I can't see very well, or something like that.
So they said to him, we've got special places for people who can't see very well.
When they came to me, I knew the German mentality.
By the way, I had boots on, high boots and bridges.
I clicked my heels, I put my things up, and I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm an Automechaniker, in-- in fluent German.
He looked at me.
He said, where are you from?
I said, Berlin.
I said, I'm an Automechaniker with-- with BMW-- Bayerische Motoren Werke.
Over there, straight away.
In the camp.
Not even a question.
I knew the mentality.
The Germans like specialists.
The Germans like working people.
They like to work.
That's [INAUDIBLE].
So I, you know, I went straight in an I was-- I was healthy anyhow.
Healthy and strong, was about half more the size that I am now.
Do you know the date you arrived at the camp?
No.
I would have to get that from Auschwitz for $25.
Yeah.
About March.
April, March.
My transport was 65,000-- 64,147.
With me were 1,500 young men coming in.
That means there were-- there were 1,500 men coming in-- young men, into Auschwitz on my transport. 64,000, 65,000, and so on.
Right?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The survivor figure of those 1,500 people are two people.
One is called [INAUDIBLE], a boxer, boxing champion for [INAUDIBLE] from Rotterdam, Holland, and me.
Because we never met anybody else in our-- in our-- in our number.
In our transport.
So they have a survivor figure after the Auschwitz came.
Approximately there are two people I knew-- I know of.
Maybe there's another one.
I don't know.
Maybe there's two.
Well, and then they took us-- you know, I think-- I think, from other interviews of other people, you know this story.
I'll make it very brief.
We were taken with trucks.
First we walked, and then we were taken on-- we walked-- no, we walked.
We walked.
Some were taken with trucks.
The trucks didn't go-- the ones who were taken on trucks were taken to the gas chambers.
I know it now.
I didn't know it then.
We-- we all walked into the camp.
Oh, about a hour.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and there we were at the barracks.
Well, in Auschwitz Stammlager we had brick.
In Birkenau, they were timber.
No.
First, we went to work, we had to be entloused.
We undressed ourselves, we went into the baths, they started to hit us with rubber hoses, they started-- everybody sat down.
Shaving.
They saved our hair of prisoners-- other prisoners did.
Oh, yeah.
We all did.
Oh, here, there, everywhere.
Because we didn't go quick enough in.
That was the initiation ceremony.
[LAUGHS] And we were in.
We were like sheep and we were shorn, quick.
We were tattooed.
Two guys hold our hand and another one tattooed us with-- with a needle like that.
Bing, bing, bing, old-fashioned one.
It was swollen.
That's right.
It was all swollen.
Shorn and tattooed.
And washed, and then Dettol.
Not Dettol, Lysol.
Lysol is a chemical substitute, like Dettol.
We were-- we were sort of-- under the arms, between the legs, it burns like hell.
We were also shorn-- our-- our pubic hair was also shorn.
And was burning like hell, because some of them [INAUDIBLE] and they made nick marks and things like that, and then you had the Lysol on top of it.
So it was hell.
Anyhow, after that, they gave us uniforms and they gave us wooden-- wooden clogs.
Beautiful.
[LAUGHTER]
Smart.
Light gray and-- and blue.
And dark blue.
Stripes.
Top and jacket.
Yeah.
Like a beret.
Like a head-- also with stripes.
And the number.
We got a number, and we had to sew it on ourselves, a number.
So I had a Star of David in yellow, and in red.
Red was political.
Down triangle, political.
A triangle on your uniform is a political.
Red is political.
Now we had the other triangle up, which was yellow, so it made it a Magen David.
And then was my number.
B, from Belgium, and then 64147.
That's what-- that was it.
I was an inmate of Auschwitz.
And then we were assigned to barracks.
The women, by the way, went all the way.
They didn't come to Auschwitz.
They came to Birkenau.
Birkenau was a woman's camp as well.
To me?
We went to the block.
I think that I was block four or something like that.
And there was a Stubendienst and a Arbeitsdienst.
That means the [INAUDIBLE] puts-- assigns you to kommandos.
And then we had to lie in the block there for-- for two, three days.
And we got a bowl of soup and a pie-- little piece of bread, and that was it.
And we lied there and lied there till-- till, in the morning, 8:00, 7:00 was Appel.
Everybody has a roll call.
Every-- everybody has to go in front.

And there was a fellow who was a Blockalteste who was in charge of the block, and he stood in fro-- you know, on the barrack, and-- and one-- one barrack was about, what, 250 people or something like that?
We were in bunks.
We were sleeping in bunks, three up.
Describe the bunks.
Very rough.
They were wood.
They were like bar-- like bunk beds, ground, middle, and top.
So I was guy on top, because I didn't want anybody to be underneath, you know?
Because some people had a bit of, you know, climbing up, climbing down, didn't go to toilets, peeing on you, you know, all sorts of things.
Straw.
Pure straw.
Straw and [INAUDIBLE].
Yes we did get blankets.
Two blankets per person.
Yes, was pretty cold, very cold.
Shivering.
We were all shivering.
We were shivering from nerves, from-- from fatigue, from under nourishment, from transport.
So, you know, it took a while to acclimatize.
Nothing.
Yes.
What our destiny is going to be, were we going to work, what happened.
And some people were hysterical.
Some people were crying, and things like that.
Yeah.
By trying to calm them down a bit, naturally, for your own sake, because you couldn't sleep otherwise.
Well, I thought, que sera, sera.
I'm going to survive it.
It doesn't matter what's going to happen.
I'm going to live.
I'm going to see those bastards die, which I did.
Every morning, 6:00.
Everybody had to come in front of the block, and then we were assigned to work.
So I was assigned on a-- the newcomers always go in out-kommandos.
That's out-- outside the camp.
Musicians.
All Jews.
Most-- mostly Jews.
And the band was playing beautiful songs and marches, and we-- we walked out.
And each Kommando walked out and the Kommandofuhrer, which was [INAUDIBLE], announced so and so many prisoners, you know?
And we walked out.
Pick and shovel.
Break stones.
Put them from there to there and from there to there.
Useless work.
About three months.
No.
Weaker and weaker.
The-- the Russians it was the same.
And you use your energy and you just, you know, get slower and slower and slower.
You try not to work when-- when they're not looking and things like that.
And I got mistreated by the foreman.
Mostly Polish foremen.
The foremen were most Poles.
None Jewish.
And they're mistreating the Jews.
They were kicking them [INAUDIBLE].
They kicked me too.
Oh, in the back, in the this, in the lips, and I kicked them back.
When the SS noticed it, they said, what's going on there?
So I stood still again because they love that, when you-- when you click your heels.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, I told the fellow to work harder-- I told him in German-- and he told me to work slower, to sabotage.
So the SS, he went, he took a bar, and-- and split his head open, from here to there.
Yeah.
No, not the guard.
The foreman, yeah.
Yeah.
So I've got them in.
I don't know.
They took him away.
We never saw him again.
Because I speak German.
He doesn't.
Oh, yeah.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH].
He couldn't speak [INAUDIBLE] German.
I said, what happened to you?
He said, well, he wanted me to work slower and I didn't want to, and he start to hit me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fluent German, so he came up to him and gave him a clock over the head.
You had to survive.
Next?
Nothing.
We worked.
Some-- some people died of exhaustion.
Yeah, sure.
I saw every day that people died.
The-- the dead people you carry on the-- on the shoulder.
You make two-- two bars, two sticks.
You put the dead, or half dead, on the shoulder, and you march back in.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We carried our dead-- in the evening, we carried our dead back in.
And the fellow who is in charge of the Kommando announces that there are 250 less 15 dead.
And they counted the dead ones and the half dead ones as well.
As you go through the gate.
Everybody had to be there.
About 10% [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because some of the-- some of the German Kapos, the more dead they brought back, more cigarettes they got from the SS.
Because they told me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After.
That's-- that's after.
They get extra rations of cigarettes if they bring a few more dead back.
They didn't kill them.
The Kapos killed them.
The Germans didn't kill them.
The Kapos killed their people.
Criminals from German jails who murdered their mother, their father, their brother, their sister, or somebody else.
Mass murders, serial murders, who have been life timers in-- in-- life timers in-- in-- in-- in security, in top security in Germany.
They took them out of there and they put them in concentration camps in charge of people.
And they were wearing a green triangle.
And they called [GERMAN], criminals.
And they [INAUDIBLE] in charge of people.
Oh, yes.
No, not criminals.
Only later.
In-- in-- in much later, there was one Kapo called Schultz.
[SIGHS] [INAUDIBLE] I worked in-- in other Kommandos, like outside.
I worked in loading trucks, loading-- loading soup, 50 gallon-- 50 liter soup, with big gloves that you don't burn your-- your feet-- your hands, loading them on trucks.
To go-- all the trucks went from the kitchen with that soup on the different Kommandos that people are working.
Delivering.
Delivery trucks.
So they had to be loaded with soup.
There were about a hundred of those 50-gallon drums with soup.
They had to be loaded like that.
I worked in that.
I worked in that.
I mean, I worked some time in cleaning.
And then I-- I-- I orgami-- I organized my-- I want to look around what was-- what there was outside the camp.
I was already a bit better then, you know.
Felt a bit better and things like that.
The normal food.
A bowl of soup which consisted of tiny, mini pieces of meat, mostly peas or beans, and a thick soup or cabbage.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then you got a quota of a black bread, and you got a tiny little-- a spoon of margarine, like you'd get here for breakfast, you know those things you got in the hotels.
And the same in jam.
And that is your daily ration.
If you do very well, you can get maybe a repeat of soup.
If you were wor-- working very well, they give you-- if there's some soup left in the-- in the-- in the thing, they give you another half.
That's it.
Then I have been approached by somebody who liked me.
I made a connection there with one of the prisoners in the fire brigade.
He was a prominent prisoner.
That means he was-- he was already in a position-- a very prominent position, let's put it that way.
No, he was not Jewish.
He was a Christian Pole.
His name was Zygmund.
Zygmund Zabolowski, and his number was 88.
That's it.
So he had a very good position there, and he knew all the Poles and all the connections in the kitchen and here and there and there and there.
So--
Oh, he wanted English lessons.
I spoke English a bit.
So I came to his barrack.
They were separate.
The fire brigade was in a separate barrack.
I heard through somebody else, or we spoke together, you know.
Somehow we met.
And I said, I can teach English.
He said, that's good.
Come to me.
And I-- I came to him where he lived.
Some people didn't like that the Jew comes.
Some Polish people didn't like that the Jew comes, and-- and that's my daughter there in the back.
Some Jew didn't like that they come in the barracks, but he didn't care so I went there and spoke to him.
And he gave me half a bread extra.
He had plenty of food.
He had access.
And-- and he always-- he liked me and we-- we became friendly, very friendly.
And there was another one, Cholonowski.
He was number 45.
He lives here in [INAUDIBLE].
Non-Jew.
Also in the fire brigade.
He was also in the fire brigade.
And I have been told some time on selections-- that means when they were selecting people in front of the block who were weak or things like that, they had to go in a truck and to be gassed.
And I knew about it before.
Always I had my information from them, from the fire brigade, that I tell others.
Some of them were very good guys.
Some of them very bad.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got onto a Kommando of butchers.
The butchers, they were working in an SS kitchen, in an SS butchery.
It was outside the camp.
They were making sausages.
Salamis and-- and Polish shortages, all for the SS, for the troops.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was introduced that I could carry them in the risk of my life, naturally.
In My life, not my-- to carry them into the camp.
They would organize all this, the butcher.
They were non-Jewish, all non-Jews.
Because I spoke good languages and I was, you know, known to be reliable and a survivor and all that.
Through connections I got that job.
SS.
To SS staff, to the commanders and all that.
Man, it's not a job.
They made me a proposition if I want to do it, to come, because they had a way of me doing it.
So I said, what the way?
He said, we put you in that, what you call Scheisskommando?
What's Scheisskomando?
[INAUDIBLE] the shit kommando.
That is emptying-- emptying the-- the latrines.
Oh, that you-- I would be hung.
Not for the SS, to bring it in.
Stolen goods.
Steal it from there and bring it into the camp.
I would be hung.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, you know, I said to myself, what have I got to lose?
My life, or this, or this, or this?
Because I was desperate for something.
Anyhow, I joined the what you call Scheisskommando.
That's a kommando were you get big gloves, big overalls, rubber boots, and you empty the latrines with a big stick.
You empty it, the outside latrines, on working places.
And you have to empty it into a container, which is a huge container drawn by four horses on a cart.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So me and another two guys went out with two guards.
Whereever we went out, we always had two armed guards with us, you know, with the rifles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one was a [NON-ENGLISH], he was-- the horses-- on the horses.
I was next to him, and another guy behind me.
Now when he came to a latrine, we stopped, we opened up the top.
It was a container.
A liquid container.
Sit down.
I know.
And we emptied the latrines and put the stuff in there.
They let the sausages into the latrine from the factory, in plastic bags.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The plastic in the latrines, in plastic bags, chained up there, let it in, and I took it out on the other side of the factory.
In the-- in the-- in the-- in the manure, in the shit inside.
In the buckets, and then the buckets into the big container who was thrown by horses into the tank.
And then we went through the gate, and I had to-- I had to-- because I spoke German, they put me up, and I had to say so and so and so, number so-and-so, number so Scheisskommando asks to come in.
And then the fellows were standing there like that, because we are all stinking.
And then he poked sticks into it.
What can he do with poking sticks into it?
He didn't find the [INAUDIBLE].
And he said, in.
Through.
And that was it.
And then on in the camp, we had to unload it into one big-- one big ditch who was-- and then we took it out and we put it in the trousers and all that and brought it back.
Washed it and brought it back to the camp, to the-- to the barracks where the butchers were.
And I got 50%.
If I got-- if I got five-- say, if I got five sausages, I got about two for myself.
There it was a fortune.
A fortune.
Absolute fortune.
I had to give to them first, otherwise I would have been dead.
I went in there, and they gave me two sausages out of five.
Were the butchers are living, in the barracks, who gave-- who gave it to me during the daytime outside.
No.
No.
You can't.
You can't.
Impossible.
They would have asked you where you got it from.
They would have talked.
You can transfer this saus-- a piece of sausage you can transfer into bread and help some-- a friend of mine with bread, but you can't give him the sausages.
They were special.
